name: Family History Interview Project
description: Interview a family member, neighbor, or older community member about their life and experiences. Prepare 5-10 open-ended questions before the interview. During the interview, take notes or (with permission) record the conversation. Afterward, analyze the interview as a primary source: What did you learn? What might the person have forgotten or remembered differently? What questions did you wish you had asked? Write or draw a summary of what this source tells you about life in a different time.
learning_objectives: Conduct an oral history interview using prepared questions
Recognize a personal interview as a primary source
Evaluate the strengths and limitations of memory as historical evidence
Summarize historical information gathered from a source
variations: Foundation (K-2): Draw pictures of what the interviewee described and dictate captions to an adult
Middle School: Interview multiple family members about the same event and compare their accounts for corroboration

name: Artifact Detective: What Can This Object Tell Us?
description: Examine a historical or unfamiliar object without being told what it is. Use structured observation: What is it made of? How big is it? Does it show signs of wear or use? What might it have been used for? Who might have used it? When might it have been made? Work through the analysis template moving from observation (what you can see) to inference (what you think it means) to questions (what you still want to know). Compare your analysis with a partner, then reveal what the object actually is. Discuss what you got right, what you got wrong, and what clues you missed.
materials: mystery objects (old tools, kitchen implements, historical reproductions, or photographs of artifacts)
learning_objectives: Practice systematic observation of physical evidence
Distinguish between observation and inference
Generate historical questions based on physical evidence
Understand that artifacts are primary sources that reveal information about the past
variations: Foundation (K-2): Use familiar objects from different decades (rotary phone, vinyl record, typewriter) and compare to modern equivalents
High School: Analyze museum catalog photographs and write formal artifact analysis reports

name: Cause-and-Effect Chain Mapping
description: Choose a historical event and create a visual cause-and-effect chain. Start with the event in the center. Map backward: What caused it? What caused that? How far back can you trace the chain? Then map forward: What were the immediate consequences? What were the long-term consequences? Use different colors for different types of causes (political, economic, social, environmental). Identify which causes were long-term conditions and which were immediate triggers. Present your chain to a partner and discuss: could removing any single cause have prevented the event?
materials: reference books or approved internet sources
learning_objectives: Identify multiple causes of a historical event
Distinguish between long-term conditions and immediate triggers
Trace consequences beyond the immediate aftermath
Evaluate the relative importance of different causal factors
variations: Elementary (3-5): Use a simple three-step chain (cause -> event -> effect) with familiar events like a local community change
High School: Add a counterfactual analysis layer -- identify the most important cause and argue what would have changed without it

name: What If? Counterfactual History Debate
description: Select a pivotal historical moment and debate what would have happened if things had gone differently. This is not wild speculation -- teams must research the actual historical context thoroughly to make plausible alternative scenarios. Each team prepares a counterfactual argument: What would change? What would stay the same? What evidence supports your scenario? Teams present their counterfactuals, then evaluate each other's reasoning. Which scenarios are most plausible and why? What does this exercise reveal about the role of contingency in history?
learning_objectives: Demonstrate deep understanding of a historical event's causal context
Construct plausible alternative scenarios grounded in evidence
Evaluate the role of contingency versus inevitability in history
Practice evidence-based argumentation and rebuttal
variations: Middle School: Focus on local or community history counterfactuals that connect to students' lived experience
College: Write a formal counterfactual analysis essay following historiographic conventions

name: Timeline Construction: My Community Through the Centuries
description: Research the history of your local community over the past 100 to 500 years. What was here before your town or neighborhood existed? Who lived here? How has the landscape changed? What buildings, roads, or landmarks survive from earlier periods? Build a physical or digital timeline that incorporates maps, photographs, written descriptions, and oral accounts from different eras. For each era on your timeline, identify at least one thing that changed and one thing that stayed the same. Present your timeline and explain which changes you think were most significant.
materials: local history books or library resources
long paper roll or digital timeline tool
learning_objectives: Research local history using multiple types of sources
Construct a chronological timeline with evidence from different periods
Identify patterns of continuity and change in a specific place
Evaluate which changes were most significant and explain why
variations: Elementary (3-5): Focus on 50-year span using family photographs and interviews with grandparents
High School: Incorporate GIS mapping or census data to show demographic and land-use change over time

name: Before and After: Comparing Eras
description: Choose a specific aspect of daily life -- food, transportation, communication, housing, work, entertainment, or schooling -- and compare it across two time periods. What was it like 100 years ago? What is it like today? What changed? What drove the change? Was the change an improvement for everyone, or did it benefit some groups while creating problems for others? Create a side-by-side comparison poster or presentation showing your findings. Include at least one primary source from each time period as evidence.
materials: reference books or approved internet sources
printed images from both time periods
learning_objectives: Compare a specific aspect of life across two historical periods
Identify what changed and what stayed the same
Explain what forces drove the changes observed
Evaluate whether change represented improvement for all groups
variations: Foundation (K-2): Compare school life today with school life when a grandparent was young using photographs and stories
High School: Compare across three or more time periods and analyze the rate and direction of change

name: Walking in Their Shoes: Historical Perspective Writing
description: Choose a historical event and write about it from the perspective of someone who experienced it but whose voice is rarely heard in traditional accounts. Not the general but the soldier, not the colonizer but the colonized, not the factory owner but the worker. This is not fiction -- it is evidence-based reconstruction. Research the actual conditions of that person's life: What did they eat? Where did they live? What were their daily concerns? What would this event have meant to them? Write a diary entry, letter, or monologue from their perspective, citing the sources you used for historical accuracy.
materials: research materials (books, primary source collections, approved websites)
learning_objectives: Research the lived experience of a specific historical group
Write from a historical perspective grounded in evidence, not imagination
Distinguish between empathy (understanding) and endorsement (agreeing)
Recognize whose voices are typically missing from historical narratives
variations: Elementary (4-5): Write a short diary entry from a child's perspective during a historical period, based on researched details about children's lives
College: Write from the perspective of someone in a different culture entirely, grappling with the methodological challenges of cross-cultural historical empathy

name: Voices of History: Same Event, Different Accounts
description: Read or listen to 3 to 4 accounts of the same historical event written by people in different social positions -- for example, a government official, a journalist, an ordinary citizen, and someone from an affected minority group. Create a comparison matrix: What does each account emphasize? What does each account omit? Where do they agree? Where do they contradict each other? Why do these differences exist? Which account do you find most trustworthy and why? Write a paragraph explaining what you can learn from comparing multiple perspectives that you could not learn from any single account alone.
materials: 3-4 primary or secondary source accounts of the same event
learning_objectives: Compare multiple accounts of the same event for similarities and differences
Identify what each perspective emphasizes and what it omits
Explain why different observers produce different accounts
Articulate the value of consulting multiple perspectives
variations: Elementary (3-5): Compare two simplified accounts of a well-known event and find three differences
High School: Include historiographic sources (scholars from different decades interpreting the same event) to show how perspective changes over time

name: Build Your Own Historical Argument
description: Choose a debatable historical question -- not 'when did X happen?' but 'why did X happen?' or 'was X inevitable?' or 'did X cause more harm than good?' Develop a clear thesis statement that takes a position on the question. Gather evidence from at least 3 sources (including at least one primary source). Construct an argument: state your thesis, present your evidence with analysis (not just quotation), address at least one counterargument, and reach a conclusion. Present your argument to peers, who evaluate its strength using a shared rubric. Revise based on feedback.
materials: research materials (books, primary source collections, approved websites)
learning_objectives: Formulate a debatable historical thesis
Select and use relevant evidence from multiple sources
Construct a structured argument with thesis, evidence, analysis, and conclusion
Address counterarguments and alternative interpretations
variations: Elementary (5): Write a paragraph arguing which cause of an event was most important, using two sources
College: Write a formal historiographic essay engaging with scholarly debate on the topic

name: Historiography Comparison: How Interpretations Change
description: Read two scholarly interpretations of the same historical topic written in different decades -- for example, how historians explained the causes of the French Revolution in the 1960s versus the 2010s, or how the history of colonial India was written in 1950 versus 2000. Compare the two interpretations: What questions does each ask? What evidence does each use? What framework or theory does each employ? What conclusions does each reach? Write an essay analyzing why the interpretations differ: What new evidence was discovered? What new questions were being asked? How did the historian's own context shape their interpretation? What does this tell you about history as an ongoing, evolving discipline?
materials: two scholarly excerpts or summaries from different decades on the same topic
learning_objectives: Compare scholarly interpretations of the same historical topic from different periods
Identify how changes in evidence, questions, and frameworks produce different interpretations
Understand that historical knowledge is constructed and evolves over time
Write a clear analysis of historiographic change
variations: Middle School: Compare two textbook accounts of the same event from different countries instead of different decades
College: Survey 4-5 interpretations across a century and trace the evolution of the scholarly conversation
